coredata.dc:title: A tailored molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on red/blue carbon dots for ultrasensitive tetracycline detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiqing
Wang, Tao
Wang, Wenjuan
Zhou, Zhiping
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor has exhibited great potential in biological and environmental detection because of the combination between the excellent selectivity of molecular imprinting technique and outstanding robustness of ratiometric measurement methods On this basis, two kinds of different well-resolved wavelengths and high-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by various solvent extraction using osmanthus fragrans leaves as carbon source. Then the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2) has been constructed by the obtained CDs which the red-emission CDs (rCDs) were used to responsive signal and the blue-emission CDs (bCDs) was served as reference signal and applied for highly selective and excellent sensitive detection of tetracycline (TC). Under optimum conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor has also exhibited lower detection limit of 1.19nM and the linear range of 0–50nM. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was further successfully employed to the determination of TC in water samples gathered from local river water and tap water, indicating its great worth toward water sample analysis in complicated environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Molecular imprinting polymer
Ultrasensitive detection
Tetracycline

Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors
Sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors
Practical Applications and Approach Performance Comparison

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for (A) preparation of rCDs and bCDs, (B) fabrication of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors for for tetracycline sensing.
TEM image of (a) rCDs (insert: size distribution histogram of rCDs), (b) bCDs (insert: size distribution histogram of bCDs), (c) bCDs@SiO2 and (d) MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2.

(a) The fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2, (b) the fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs of NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2, (c) the linear relationships between MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 (black curve) and NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 (red curve) and (d) fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs of MIPs@rCD.

The linear relationships of (a) MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 and (b) NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 with addition of same concentrations (0–50nM) of TC and analogs (insert: corresponding photographs of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 and NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 with 50nM TC and analogs).
The results for the quantitative determination of TC in tap water and river water samples.
Comparison of the various fluorescence sensors for TC detection in different samples.

head.ce:title: A tailored molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on red/blue carbon dots for ultrasensitive tetracycline detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province, 212013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two kinds of well-resolved wavelengths carbon dots (CDs) including red-emission CDs (rCDs) and blue-emission CDs (bCDs) were fabricated using osmanthus fragrans leaves as carbon source through solvent extraction methods. The prepared bCDs as reference signal and rCDs as response signal were constructed the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs@bCDs@SiO2 sensor by the precipitation polymerization process for ultrasensitive and high selective TC determination.

The molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor has exhibited great potential in biological and environmental detection because of the combination between the excellent selectivity of molecular imprinting technique and outstanding robustness of ratiometric measurement methods On this basis, two kinds of different well-resolved wavelengths and high-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by various solvent extraction using osmanthus fragrans leaves as carbon source. Then the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2) has been constructed by the obtained CDs which the red-emission CDs (rCDs) were used to responsive signal and the blue-emission CDs (bCDs) was served as reference signal and applied for highly selective and excellent sensitive detection of tetracycline (TC). Under optimum conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor has also exhibited lower detection limit of 1.19nM and the linear range of 0–50nM. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was further successfully employed to the determination of TC in water samples gathered from local river water and tap water, indicating its great worth toward water sample analysis in complicated environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel type of fluorescent nanoparticles, have received tremendous interests compared with traditional fluorescent dyes and heavy metal quantum dots due to their distinguished properties including robust chemical stability, simple synthetic routes, excellent environment friendly and low toxicity [1–4]. Recently, the extensive capability of CDs has been exploited in different fields of sensing [5,6], biomedicine [7], bio-imaging [8,9], catalysis [10] and solar cells [11,12]. The fluorescent CDs are generally made from organic compounds (e.g. citric acid, glycol, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and glucose), large carbon materials (e.g. carbon nanotubes and activate carbon) and natural materials (e.g. milk, egg, hair, honey and fruit juice) [13–15]. Among them, natural raw materials are extensively acknowledged to be a kind of green, simple and low-cost precursors to fabricate CDs [16,17]. Moreover, most natural materials retain complex component to make CDs hold some surface functional groups and special properties [16].

Important investigation based on the potential of CDs fluorescent sensors is still required to explore. Especially the single wavelength-based fluorescent CDs sensor can be easily disturbed by the variable environmental conditions, so that the fluctuation of excitation source and the inaccuracy probe concentration have been seriously limited their applications. However, the fluorescent ratiometric sensing can effectively eliminate the deviation to ameliorate the detection accuracy through measuring the fluorescence intensity ratios from two or more emission bands with different wavelengths [18,19]. Recently, the development of fluorescent ratiometric sensors has attracted enormous attentions because of high sensitivity and fast visual detection for target, such as antibiotics, proteins, pesticides and so on [20–22]. But the fluorescent ratiometric sensors is urgent to improve the detection selectivity toward target.

To ameliorate the selectivity of fluorescent ratiometric sensors, molecular imprinting technique (MIT) is an effective and widespread tactic by the fabrication of molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs) with customized recognition sites complementary in shape, size, and functional group to the template molecules [23–25]. Due to the combination of MIPs and fluorescent ratiometric sensors displayed apparent advantages, there are many reports about the construction of molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) based fluorescent ratiometric sensors. For example, Wang’s group prepared a molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the application of visually detecting bovine hemoglobin [26]. Zhang’s group was constructed the molecularly imprinted polymer coated quantum dots ratiometric fluorescence probe for the selective detection of mitoxantrone [27]. The above mentioned ratiometric fluorescence sensors are strikingly enhance the selectivity but they are based on the CdTe quantum dots which has greatly caused environmental pollutions. Thus, establishing an environment-friendly molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the excellent selective and highly sensitive detection of targets especially pollutions would make incredible progress to protect environments.

Tetracycline (TC), as a type of versatile broad-spectrum bacteriostatic antibiotic, has widely used to treat a variety of nonviral infections because it can not only kill bacteria but also has excellent inhibition and sterilization effects toward other pathogenic microorganisms [28–30]. Apart from the enormous use in aquaculture, TC is extensively committed to the treatment of human disease, breeding as a drug additive for the prevention of intestinal infection in animal, growth-promotion [31,32]. Unfortunately, the most frequently use TC for the prevention and treatment of animal infection has resulted in the raised levels of resistant antibiotic residues in water, which could be transferred to humans through the food chain and would be detrimental for physical health including yellowing of teeth, allergic reactions, liver damage and even gastrointestinal disturbance [32–34]. Therefore, the demand of ultra-sensitively determining the content of TC has dramatically increased.

Comprehensive account of the above mentioned, two kinds of well-resolved wavelengths CDs using osmanthus fragrans leaves as carbon source were fabricated through solvent extraction methods. Then the highly fluorescent CDs were used to construct a molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the high selective and ultra-sensitive detection of TC. To our best knowledge, there is a few studies used different resolved wavelengths CDs obtained from the same natural materials to fabricated MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which applied for the determination of TC. Moreover, the synthesized MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor was testified to sensitively and selectively detect TC by measuring the real sample concentrations of TC and have achieved satisfied results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process and visual principle of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor were displayed in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the red-emission and blue-emission CDs were fabricated by different solutions extraction using the osmanthus fragrans leaves as carbon source. Then for separation of the response signal rCDs and reference signal bCDs, the bCDs were carried out embedding process and coated a silica layer by Stöber method [35]. Moreover, the silica layer could effectively inhibit direct interaction between bCDs and TC to present a trustworthy reference signal for the ratiometric fluorescence detection. Subsequently, the imprinting molecular polymer of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor was prepared on the silica layer of the CDs by the co-condensation reaction in presence of rCDs, template of TC, functional monomers of AM and cross-linker of EGDMA to construct the specific recognition system through hydrogen bonding. Due to the presence of TC, the fluorescence of rCDs was quickly quenched while the fluorescence of bCDs was still protected. Correspondingly, the visual fluorescence of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor has been changed from purplish to bright blue under a UV lamp. After eluting the template of TC, the polymer layer was obtained the tailored binding sites of specific size, shape and functional groups consistent with TC. At the same time, the fluorescence of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor has shown purplish under a UV lamp due to the recovery of rCDs fluorescence. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors were rebound to the binding site rapidly with the template molecules to display the visually detection results by the color changes from purplish to blue under a UV lamp.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of bCDs and rCDs were displayed in Fig. S1b CDs exhibited two obvious absorption peaks at 240nm and 354nm are assigned to the π–π* transition by sp2 conjugated extensions and n–π* transition of the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface [36,37]. Due to the rCDs exist different and several complicated surface functional states, there are several different absorption peaks at 271nm, 308nm, 398nm and 656nm, respectively. The peak at 271nm was attributed to π–π* transition of C–C bonds, the absorption peaks located at 308nm and 398nm were ascribed to n–π* transition of the presence of C–O or C–N. There may be the charge transfer in-between the metal-ligand bonding resulted in the absorbance peak at 656nm because of the presence of trace metals in sosmanthus fragrans leave [38].

To ensure perfect property of the prepared rCDs and bCDs, we have optimized the ratio between extraction solutions and carbon sources and time of the factors. As shown in Fig. S2a, the fluorescence intensity of rCDs has been optimum with the ratio was 60mL ethanol and 1.0g carbon sources. When the ethanol was increased from 15mL to 60mL, the fluorescence intensity of obtained rCDs was subsequently enhanced, but gradually decreased with the adding more than 60mL ethanol. Moreover, the bCDs has been exhibited the strongest fluorescence at the ratio was 60mL 1% PEI solutions and 1.0g carbon sources (Fig. S2b), and the changes between the fluorescence intensity and volume were similar to the rCDs. Fig. S3a-b have separately revealed the fluorescence intensity both of rCDs and bCDs were gradually enhanced with the time from 12h to 48h, and hardly obvious changes with time over 48h. Thus, the 48h was set as the optimal reaction time for preparing both rCDs and bCDs.

The morphological structures of rCDs, bCDs, bCDs@SiO2 and MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 were respectively carried out by TEM. As display in Fig. 1 a and b, the rCDs and bCDs particles were nearly spherical and well-dispersed with diameters of 1.8±0.4nm and 3.4±0.3nm. However, both of the rCDs and bCDs were displayed no apparent crystal lattice and indicated an amorphous structure. Fig. 1c has revealed the bCDs@SiO2 showed the uniform spherical and the diameter has about 50nm and bCDs were coated by silica layer. After surface imprinted polymerization, it can be obviously seen that MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 has constructed by the bCDs@SiO2 core and polymer nanoshell and the diameter has markedly enlarged to 170±20nm (Fig. 1d). These results all indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor has been prepared successfully.

As we all known that the polymer thickness of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 has tremendous impacts on the recognition performance to TC. We also investigated the molar ratio among template, functional monomers and cross-linker changed from 1:1 to 1:0.1 (Table S1 and Fig. S4 a–d) and found that the polymer layer has thickened gradually and the monodisperse and morphology were changed correspondingly. Comprehensively consider the stability and recognition of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensor, we have chosen the ratio of 1:0.6 as the experimental optimal conditions to ensure a certain amount of recognition sites and faster combined with TC on surface of sensor.

To confirmed the fluorescence property of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors, the fluorescence emission spectra has been explored and displayed in Fig. S5a. Before removing TC, the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors has shown a relative strong blue-emission fluorescence and a weak red-emission fluorescence. Correspondingly, the fluorescence of the suspension was lean towards violet under a UV lamp. But the red-emission fluorescence has been recovered swiftly after eluting the template from the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors and the suspension under a UV lamp has been converted to purplish red, which was similar to the ratiometric fluorescence NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors. Therefore, we have drawn the conclusion that the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors have an excellent recognition to template of TC for visual detection.

The FTIR spectroscopy was applied to observe the imprinting process during eluting and rebinding of TC to the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors. From Fig. S4b, it can be seen that the broad absorption bands at 806cm−1 and 1101cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of Si–O and Si–O–Si, respectively. These above-mentioned peaks manifested the SiO2 matrices have been existed in the MIPs. The stretching vibrations of C–O at 1730cm−1are attributed to the cross-linker EGDMA. The stretching vibrations of N–H and C–N are the characteristic absorption of 1610cm−1 and 3438cm−1 which caused by the functional monomer of AM [39]. Therefore, the presence of these peaks further demonstrated that the AM were successfully embedded and the template can effectively rebind to the binding sites in the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors.

The elemental composition of the fabricated ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors were analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S5a has displayed the XPS spectrum of C 1s of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors which has been divided into three distinct peaks at 284.39eV, 286.20eV and 288.46eV, which are attributed to C–C/C–C, C–N/C–O and C–O [40]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S5b) was accorded with C–O (532.00eV), Si–O (532.56eV) and C–O (533.10eV), respectively [30]. These existed nitrogen-involving bonds were proved by N 1s signal in the XPS spectrum which were including C–N–C (399.83eV) and C–NH2 (400.55eV) (as shown in Fig. S5c) [41]. Fig. S5d revealed the Si 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum has been separated two peaks at 102.98eV and 103.44eV, corresponding to Si–O/SiO2 and Si–C [42]. These results have matched with the FT-IR data and further illustrated the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Some critical factors including pH, stability and response time has tremendous impacts on the recognition performance of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors. The fluorescence stability of the four samples (rCDs, bCDs, bCDs@SiO2 and MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2) have also been measured and displayed in Fig. S6a. The four samples have maintained a strong fluorescent intensity for 10 weeks in air at room temperature which demonstrated the four samples have a wonderful stability to resist the neutral external environments. Meanwhile, the response time of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors to TC has been investigated to clarify interactions with the temporal evolution (Fig. S6b). The red-emission fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors has been continuously decreased and then retained after 6.0min with the additions of 30nM TC, and the blue-emission fluorescence intensity has not apparently changed. This phenomenon has interpreted that the thoroughly interactions between the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors and TC needed 6.0min. Hence, the optimal response time has been determined to 6.0min for the following measurements. In order to explored the fluorescent properties of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors at different pH value, the rCDs, bCDs and bCDs@SiO2 have been made a comparison at the same measuring conditions (Fig. S6c and Fig. S6d). It has been apparently distinctions that the fluorescent intensities of rCDs and bCDs have been sharply weakened during the solutions pH value was less 6.0 or over 8.0, but the bCDs@SiO2 and the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors could keep favorable fluorescent intensities in the range of 5.0–10. These results have revealed that the polymer layer of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors or silica layer of bCDs@SiO2 could make an outstanding surface defense to be adapted the pH value variations from weak acid to alkaline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors have emitted two well-distinguished emission peaks focused on 445nm and 672nm. With the increasing addition of TC, the red-emission fluorescence intensity at 672nm has gradually decreased but the blue-emission fluorescence at 445nm kept almost unchanged (Fig. 2 a). Due to the bCDs as reference signal and the rCDs as recognition signals could be quenched by TC and the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors has created apparent color variations which was changed from red to blue under a UV lamp for visual detection. To illustrate the superiority of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors, the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors were also determined similarly and shown in Fig. 2b. The blue-emission fluorescence at 445nm was the same as ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors to keep almost unchanged, the red-emission fluorescence intensity at 672nm has also been decreased but not obviously than ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors along with the same addition of TC. Correspondingly, the slight quenched fluorescence could not cause distinguished colors variations by the naked eye detection.

As shown in Fig. 2c, a fitting linear curve equation was log[(I672/I445)0/(I672/I445)]=0.01869 (CTC/nM)−7.9577×10−7 (R1 2 =0.99582), which was a good linear relationship acquired by the ratio fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors was closely related to the concentrations of TC in the range of 0–50nM. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) was down to 1.19nM and defined by 3δ/s, which δ signifies the standard deviation of the blank signals and s means the slope of the calibration curve. Compared with MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2, the NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 has displayed lower sensitivity and narrower linear range to TC. This phenomenon was owing to the absence of specific recognition site in the ratiometric fluorescence NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors and the TC could not invade the NIP inner to interact with rCDs.

With the purpose of demonstrating the advantages that the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors for onsite naked-eye detection of TC is practical, red-emission of MIPs@rCDs was also evaluated under the same conditions. Fig. 2d has shown that the fluorescence of the MIPs@rCDs has been progressively quenched along with increasing the concentration of TC, but the color change of the MIPs@rCDs fluorescence quenching was difficult to distinguish the diversities under a UV lamp for the naked eye detection. Therefore, the comparison study verified that the ratiometric fluorescence sensors exhibited superior sensitivity and reliability to TC than the single fluorescence.

The effects of metal cations (including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+ and Zn2+) on the fluorescence detection of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 to TC were also investigated in this experiment. From Fig. S8 it can be seen that only Hg2+ and Ag+ have slightly quenched to fluorescence intensity of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2, but they and other cations have hardly caused effect on the results of MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 determined TC because of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors could effectively eliminate the deviation to ameliorate the detection accuracy.

The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors and NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors was evaluated. The linear relationship among TC and other template analogs (DC, CTC, AMO and CAP) with the concentrations range 0–50nmol of sensors was explored. From Fig. 3 a, it has been found that the linear relationship of TC to MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors was strikingly higher than analogs. The template of TC has maximum quenching effect to the red-emission fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors with the comparison of other template analogs. Corresponding, the obvious color changes have happened in ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors after adding 50nM TC under the UV lamp. But only little changes have taken place in MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors with 50nM analogs. This phenomenon mainly caused by a plenty of tailored recognition sites to TC which existed in the imprinting polymeric layer and the added TC could quickly rebind with imprinting holes. The red-emission fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors was heavily decreased by the additions of TC but the blue-emission fluorescence has unchanged which bCDs have been protected by silica layers. However, the same amount of other template analogs has only a little quenching to the fluorescence of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors. On the contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescence NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors was also investigated at the same conditions and acquired another linear relationship (Fig. 3b). It has only little decreased fluorescence intensity of NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors to TC and other analogs, and the quenching effects to NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors were almost no difference. Meanwhile, the color changes were not obviously from NIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 with 50nm TC or analogs under UV lamp. The results implied that the MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 had high selectivity and specific cavities of the same shape and size as TC but not matched the analogs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical Applications and Approach Performance Comparison
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical application of the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors towards TC, some studies were further investigated in tap water and river water. Owing to TC was not detected in the tap water using the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors, a recovery measurement was carried out. It was clearly presented in Table 1 that the obtained recoveries were 99.92–100.36% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were varied from 0.62% to 0.95% in tap water samples, and the recoveries ranged from 99.13% to 102.75% with RSD of 0.87–0.98% in river water samples. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors has fully displayed the practically applicable and dependable for sensitive and precise detection of low TC contents in real samples.

The comparison between our established the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors and other reported methods for the detection of TC has been listed in Table 2 . Although the LOD of the MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors was 1.19nM and not the lowest in Table 2, the most compared fluorescence sensors for TC have been developed with only one fluorescence emission wavelength and the application of naked-eye detection has been limited to a large extent [43]. Moreover, the previous methods required elaborate technique or complicated operations while our proposed the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors has the advantages of low-cost instrumentation, simple operation and rapid response. So, we developed the ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors could be well applied for sensitive and selective detection of TC in complex samples.

In this work, we have firstly successfully presented the fabrication and application of a novel ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors for the determination of TC based on two kinds of different resolution wavelength CDs. The red-emission and blue-emission CDs were prepared by different solvent extraction from osmanthus fragrans leaves at room temperature. The bCDs were used as reference signal by wrapped in SiO2 layer to prevent the direct interaction between bCDs and TC, and the responsive signal of rCDs which could be quenched by TC were embedded in molecular imprinting polymer layer. The ratiometric fluorescence MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 sensors has displayed excellent selectivity, high sensitivity and reliable visual determination and presented a practical application for the detection of TC in complex environment with a desirable result. However, further investigations of the potential developments of this approach for detection and analysis other targets in real samples need to explore.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21507045 and No. 21607013), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160569), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX18_2232).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2018.12.007.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
